Optimal DNN training via (stochastic, normalized) gradient ascent: Geometric intuition.  Consider the network in Figure 5a, with all link capacities 1, and each node pair (1,2), (1,3), and (2,3) connected by the single permissible path in the figure. The realized demand matrix D(t) for all time intervals t is identical, with demands d12=d13=d23=1 and all other demands 0. We examine how the total flow F is impacted by value assignments vp≥0 to the permissible paths. Let v(1,2), v(1,3), and v(2,3) denote the values associated with paths (1,2), (1,3), and (2,3), respectively. Fixing v(2,3)=1, we visualize the total flow F as a function of v(1,2) and v(1,3) in Figure 5b.

A geometric interpretation of concavity is that the graph of a function lies on or above the straight line (chord) connecting any two points on the graph. As shown in Figure 5b, the total flow F is not concave in the path values, as the graph of the function lies below the red line. We show, however, that the total flow is quasiconcave, meaning the function’s values along the line between any two points do not drop below the smaller of the two. This is visually evident for points A and B: All function values on the red line exceed the lowest value (point A). Quasiconcavity allows for efficient optimization via normalized gradient ascent.26 In our case, starting with arbitrary values for v(1,2) and v(1,3), we can apply (normalized) gradient ascent to reach the maximum flow at point B.

We prove that the quasiconcavity of the total flow extends to any network topology and choice of permissible paths,27 so normalized gradient ascent always converges to the maximum flow. This extends to virtually all other well-studied flow-optimization objectives, including maximum concurrent flow and congestion minimization, and to probabilistic, time-varying demands. Indeed, building on results in ML theory,8,17 we show that an idealized training process via stochastic normalized gradient ascent29 yields optimal performance. To handle the nondifferentiability of certain objectives, our analysis relies on subgradient updates. See Perry et al.27 for a detailed exposition of the idealized training process, and for our theoretical results. These theoretical results provide a foundation for the empirical results discussed below, which were obtained for an implementation of DOTE that approximates this idealized training process.

Closed-form (sub)gradients.  DNN training in DOTE leverages another key structural property: Unlike many optimization settings that rely on approximations to estimate gradients, our setting admits a closed-form expression, enabling more efficient optimization. The intuition is that once a demand matrix is realized, the induced performance for all possible flow configurations—not just the one actually chosen—can be computed. Revisiting the example in Figure 5a, for any realized demand matrix D(t), the total flow F as a function of the path values is explicitly computable as: min{v(1,2)γ,d12}+min{v(1,3)γ,d13}+min{v(2,3)γ,d23}, where γ is the normalization factor applied to DNN-outputted path values. While this expression for F is not differentiable at some points (where two or more arguments of a minimum coincide), it is subdifferentiable everywhere. Thus, one can directly compute subgradients with respect to the path values without resorting to approximations. This property generalizes to arbitrary networks.27

Leveraging recent advances in large-scale DNN training.  DNN training in our implementation of DOTE builds on recent developments in large-scale optimization, namely, the Adam stochastic gradient descent optimizer,15 which incorporates momentum and an adaptive step size. This accommodates efficient training on extensive empirical data (many tens of thousands of traffic-demand matrix snapshots in our experiments).

Evaluation

Using network topologies and traffic demands from production networks, including Microsoft’s, we sought to answer the following questions: How does DOTE compare against an omniscient oracle with perfect knowledge of future demands? And how does DOTE stack up against predict-and-optimize flow-optimization methods in backbone networks in terms of solution quality and runtime efficiency?

We evaluated DOTE against predict-and-optimizec using empirically derived historical datasets from three production networks: GEANT,30 Abilene,1 and Microsoft’s private backbone network (MBB). Traffic on MBB includes both datacenter-to-datacenter and external customer traffic. To analyze performance by traffic class, we also considered MBBDC, which includes only datacenter-to-datacenter traffic. Together, these empirical datasets span network topologies of varying sizes, demand matrices with different levels of predictability, and differing amounts of available training data (from weeks to months). The above-mentioned networks were supplemented by the KDL network from Topology Zoo16 to assess runtimes. Table 2 summarizes topology sizes and traffic granularity. For each topology, we evaluated two node-interconnection schemes: k-shortest paths (k=8) and edge-disjoint paths. (See Perry et al.27 for more details about our evaluation framework.)

Solution quality.  Figure 6 shows results for the maximum-flow objective across all four network datasets and the two path-generation schemes, normalized by the total flow achieved by an omniscient oracle with perfect future traffic knowledge. For each network, 75% of demand matrices are used for training, and 25% for testing. Candlesticks display performance distributions: boxes represent the 25th to 75th percentiles, whiskers show the full range, and average and median values are also plotted. Strikingly, DOTE approaches oracle performance even in the lower percentiles, consistently outperforming predict-and-optimize. On Microsoft’s datasets, DOTE delivers roughly 10% more traffic on average than predict-and-optimize, a significant improvement in network utilization. As expected, the gap between DOTE and predict-and-optimize is more pronounced for all Microsoft traffic (MBB) compared to the more predictable inter-datacenter traffic (MBBDC). DOTE maintains high network utilization even at the very lowest percentiles, while predict-and-optimize lags in tail performance across all evaluated scenarios.

Runtimes.  Table 3 compares the online runtimes of the two flow-optimization approaches. Observe that DOTE’s DNN inference time is significantly faster than predict-and-optimize’s LP-based optimization. LP computation latency can be minutes on very large networks.2,25 Indeed, solving the LP for the large KDL network (see Table 2) lasts close to two minutes. In contrast, DOTE’s DNN inference latency on large networks, such as KDL, is still within a few seconds.

We refer the reader to Perry et al.27 for many more results, covering additional flow-optimization objectives (congestion minimization and maximum concurrent flow), network topologies and traffic scenarios, path-assignment schemes, and comparison baselines (oblivious routing, reinforcement learning, and hybrid approaches). Also see that paper for results on robustness to network failures, sensitivity to unexpected changes in traffic and to natural demand drift, and training-time analysis.

Conclusion

In this article, we introduced a novel algorithmic perspective on bridging a major gap between the theory and practice of flow optimization in computer networks. We laid down the theoretical foundations for our methodology, and realized our method by leveraging recent advances in large-scale optimization and deep learning. Using extensive real-world data, we established that our approach substantially surpasses the state of the art in terms of both flow solution quality and online runtimes. Our work leaves many exciting open avenues for future research on extending and applying DOTE. Promising directions include applying machine learning to cope with network failures, learning the permissible network paths, and leveraging DOTE’s fast DNN-inference-based computation for network capacity planning.
item.dc:creator: Srikanth Kandula
Ishai Menache
Michael Schapira
Aviv Tamar

item.title: Redefining the Software Engineering Profession for AI
item.dc:creator: Mark Russinovich and Scott Hanselman
item.description: A preceptor program that pairs early-in-career developers with experienced mentors in real product teams ensures today’s expertise becomes tomorrow’s intuition.
item.content:encoded:
Generative AI has fractured the economics of software engineering. Agentic coding assistants now give senior engineers an AI boost, multiplying their throughput, while imposing an AI drag on early-in-career (EiC) developers who lack the judgment and context to steer, verify, and integrate AI output. The result is a new incentive structure: Hire seniors, automate juniors. But without EiC hiring, the profession’s talent pipeline collapses, and organizations face a future without the next generation of experienced engineers.

Our thesis is simple: We must keep hiring EiC developers, accept that they initially reduce capacity, and deliberately design systems that make their growth an explicit organizational goal. The path forward is a culture of preceptorship at scale. We must enable senior mentorship with AI systems that capture reasoning, surface misconceptions, and turn daily work into teachable moments for EiC developers. This article explores how such systems can close the training gap and preserve the craft of software engineering in the age of AI.

The AI Boost

The past year has marked a sharp turning point in software engineering productivity. Agentic coding assistants—systems that interpret goals, reason across repositories, and iteratively generate, test, and refine code—are reshaping what small teams can achieve. Internal data and independent studies now show that experienced developers using these tools can complete complex tasks several times faster, with order-of-magnitude improvements increasingly common.

In Microsoft’s Project Societas, the project name for their new Office Agent, seven part-time engineers delivered a consumer-ready preview in just 10 weeks, producing more than 110,000 lines of code that was 98% AI-generated. Human work shifted from authoring to directing: specifying goals, verifying correctness, and integrating the agentic output into a coherent system.

Aspire is another large system that shows how this transformation unfolds in practice and changes how engineering teams work. Teams moved through distinct phases, first using chat assistants locally, then allowing coding agents to open pull requests, and eventually operating in human-agent swarms where every pull-request (PR) was shippable and review became a shared dialogue between people and machines. The work happened in long GitHub PRs where senior engineers discuss the architectural goals while the coding agent provides solutions. The result was a faster feedback loop, higher parallelism, and drastically lower opportunity cost for experimentation.

The agentic engineering intern.  While AI is boosting software development, examples of frontier coding agents exhibiting intern-like behaviors demonstrate their limitations and how an EiC developer might have difficulty spotting or guiding the agents away from sub-optimal designs and erroneous conclusions.

In Figure 1, the agent has inserted a sleep into code that was crashing because of a race condition. This type of change only masks an underlying complex synchronization bug, but an EiC developer might consider it an effective fix if the race no longer surfaces in tests.

The agent even has trouble explaining its rationale for inserting the delay, which does not actually reduce the risk of the race in this case. Upon being challenged, it admits its reasoning was flawed (Figure 2), but AI can also conclude correct reasoning is wrong when challenged by a user’s suggestions that it might be incorrect.

Only an engineer familiar with synchronization protocols, the synchronization primitives in use, and the architecture of the code can have the confidence to point out the agent’s mistakes and have the insights necessary to guide it in a correct direction.

Progress in many of these cases requires the user to tell the agent how to proceed. In Figure 3, for example, the user guides the agent to insert sleeps that will induce the code to exhibit a race condition for more reliable debugging.

There are dozens of examples like this from multiple agentic AI projects that show the model claiming success when the code had significant bugs, implementing inefficient algorithms, duplicating common code throughout the code base, dismissing crashes and hangs as not relevant to the task at hand, leaving debug code behind, taking shortcuts with hacks that make code work for specific tests but that don’t generalize, and more.

Although AI agents are advancing rapidly, human expertise remains essential in software development. Programming is not software engineering. Even the most reliable systems cannot fully replace the judgment, creativity, and adaptability required to handle uncertainty, make complex decisions, and maintain security. While agents can speed up workflows and reduce manual effort, they lack the intuition to anticipate edge cases and build robust solutions. Relying too much on AI risks missing subtle bugs, architectural flaws, and vulnerabilities that only skilled engineers can catch. Human oversight, critical thinking, and domain knowledge are indispensable for both correcting errors and driving innovation as technology progresses.

The narrowing pyramid hypothesis.  Traditional software engineering organizations hire EiC developers to augment the capacity of the organization by having them take on relatively simple bug fixes and coding tasks. In performing these tasks, they gain experience and become familiar with the coding standards of a project, as well as its architecture, implementation, build, and test systems. Some of them with the desire and capability rise to become tech leads, which own more complex tasks that span broader portions of a system and delegate tasks to the EiCs. Ratios of EiCs to leads are commonly on the order of 10:1.

Generative AI currently acts as seniority-biased technological change: It disproportionately amplifies engineers who already possess systems judgment, like taste for architecture, debugging under uncertainty, and operational intuition. EiC developers who lack hard-won systems knowledge will struggle to contribute in an AI-driven environment. Labor data shows that after GPT-4’s release, employment of 22–25-year-olds in highly AI-exposed jobs (like software development) fell by roughly 13%, even as senior roles grew. A recent study from Harvard, “Generative AI as Seniority-Biased Technological Change: Evidence from U.S. Résumé and Job Posting Data”,3 observes that AI seems to already be creating a form of “seniority-biased technological change”

AI is amplifying senior talent but risks leaving new talent behind, creating a lopsided organization and a shrinking “base of the pyramid.” The old model of large teams of mid-level/junior developers adding incremental features is now under economic pressure. Left unchecked, fewer EiCs will gain “systems taste,” architectural intuition, and operational savvy—eroding code quality and slowing innovation. Ethan Mollick observes in his post, “On Working with Wizards”:

…We need to become connoisseurs of output rather than process. We need to curate and select among the outputs the AI provides, but more than that, we need to work with AI enough to develop instincts for when it succeeds and when it fails. We have to learn to judge what’s right, what’s off, and what’s worth the risk of not knowing. This creates a hard problem for education: How do you train someone to verify work in fields they haven’t mastered, when the AI itself prevents them from developing mastery? Figuring out how to address this gap is increasingly urgent. 2

The solution is not to assume EiCs will benefit from the same productivity gains as seniors, but to deliberately hire and invest in them. That means giving them direct exposure to debugging, design trade-offs, implementation, and build systems—the fundamentals needed to critically evaluate AI output.

Practitioners must grow when exposed to AI or this is all for naught. Again, Ethan Mollick:

…every time we hand work to a wizard, we lose a chance to develop our own expertise; to build the very judgment we need to evaluate the wizard’s work. We’re getting something magical, but we’re also becoming the audience rather than the magician, or even the magician’s assistant. 2

The new model must allow both seniors and juniors, experienced alongside early in career, to learn, not just produce. Senior mentors should assess weaknesses and guide focus areas, while AI serves as an accelerant, not a crutch.

The preceptor program.  To meet the challenge of developing EiC developers in an AI-driven environment, we propose a preceptor program that pairs EiC developers directly with experienced mentors in real product teams. Preceptors guide and grow practitioners, teaching them how to direct agentic AI tools, develop critical judgment, and learn the production function of senior engineers. This approach ensures that learning, not just throughput, is a core part of engineering in the age of AI.

Research from MIT in early 2025 observed “cognitive debt” in adults who used ChatGPT to write SAT-style essays, noting reduced brain activity compared to those who wrote unaided, as well as lower recall minutes afterward.1 Direct engagement is associated with more effective learning outcomes. By training EiC developers specifically for an AI-powered environment—learning fundamentals, understanding AI’s strengths and weaknesses, and developing judgment about when to trust or override—we preserve the long-term health of our engineering workforce. This intentional investment keeps the pyramid strong from base to peak, but with a base that is focused on refreshing senior talent rather than augmenting the productivity of the organization.

For AI-accelerated teams, the principles of judgment and sensitivity to “code smell” become essential. EiC developers should not be shielded from the problem-solving process; they should be invited into all aspects, helping with prompting, debugging, and reviewing alongside their mentors so they can see how expertise interacts with the AI. Their contribution is not raw velocity but learning in context: surfacing misconceptions, asking why the agent’s output fails, and gradually internalizing the reasoning their preceptors already take for granted. Senior engineer preceptors, in turn, have a responsibility to externalize their senior judgment, helping turn expertise into teachable moments with the goal of converting the “AI drag” of inexperience into the next generation’s capacity for discernment.

Preceptorship carries a deliberate, professional weight: It conveys both assessment and accountability. It frames software engineering not as a fading craft in the era of AI, but as a profession where senior engineers have a responsibility to guide those just beginning their practice. Preceptors form a trained subset of the senior pool, each capable of mentoring three to five EiCs. With an effectively unlimited inference budget, these pairs can experiment freely—starting small, iterating quickly, learning continuously, and scaling as the program matures.

To support learners and provide information to preceptors, coding assistants may benefit from an explicit EiC mode that defaults to Socratic coaching before code generation. Andrej Karpathy, in a recent interview, mentioned: “[As an educator,] I’m not going to present the solution before you guess. That would be wasteful…to present you with the solution before I give you a shot to try to come up with it yourself.” The coding assistant, much like Khan Academy’s Khanmigo does for math and science, should challenge the learner, explain its code-generation process, quiz the learner on key concepts and decisions, and actively track their strengths and weaknesses throughout their interactions.

Preceptors should be able to review chat logs from learners to monitor progress, provide focused guidance, and address knowledge gaps. This ensures assistants support not just code generation but also foster learning and effective mentorship.

The ideal learner-to-preceptor ratio is estimated to be between 3:1 and 5:1, depending on software complexity, learner experience, and preceptor involvement. Programs are expected to run for at least a year, possibly longer, based on needed skills and product complexity.

Conclusion

Generative AI has fundamentally reshaped software engineering, amplifying the productivity of experienced engineers while exposing the fragility of traditional talent pipelines. If organizations focus only on short-term efficiency—hiring those who can already direct AI—they risk hollowing out the next generation of technical leaders. Sustaining the discipline requires intentional design for growth: embedding structured mentorship and preceptorship into daily work, and equipping AI systems to teach through Socratic dialogue and guided reasoning.

The future of software engineering will be defined not by the volume of code AI can generate but by how effectively humans learn, reason, and mature alongside these systems. Investing in early-in-career developers through deliberate preceptorship ensures today’s expertise becomes tomorrow’s intuition. In balancing automation with apprenticeship, we preserve the enduring vitality of the software engineering profession.
item.dc:creator: Scott Hanselman

item.title: Evolving the ACM Digital Library
item.dc:creator: Jack Davidson and Wayne Graves
item.description: The transition to open access, Basic, and Premium is not a completed project—it is an evolving framework.
item.content:encoded:
The ACM Digital Library has long been central to how computing knowledge is disseminated, discovered, and preserved. Over time, it has come to serve a broad audience that includes researchers, educators, students, practitioners, librarians, institutions, and authors spanning every region of the world and every subdiscipline of computing.

In recent years, expectations around scholarly communication have shifted significantly. Open access has moved from an emerging model to a core requirement for many authors, institutions, and funding agencies. At the same time, users increasingly expect modern discovery tools, richer signals of value, and platforms that support a wide range of research, teaching, and professional workflows.

In response to these changes, ACM undertook a multi-year effort to transition the Digital Library to a fully open-access foundation. That transition had two tightly coupled components: moving to full open access and introducing a new service model with two tiers—Basic and Premium. When these changes were introduced, they generated substantial discussion within the community, including concern and, in some cases, strong pushback.

This article reflects on that transition and what we learned along the way. We explain why the changes were undertaken, how they were developed, what we heard from the community, how that feedback shaped subsequent decisions, and how we see the Digital Library continuing to evolve. Our aim is both explanatory and reflective, sharing lessons learned while reaffirming a commitment to transparency and ongoing engagement.

Why Change the Digital Library Model

The move toward open access did not arise from a single decision or moment. Rather, it emerged from the growing difficulty of reconciling several competing concerns within a single, monolithic model.

Authors increasingly require publication venues that comply with funder and institutional open-access mandates. Libraries desire sustainable and predictable approaches to access and cost. Readers expect immediate, barrier-free access to research results. At the same time, the Digital Library continued to grow in scale and complexity, supporting new content types, richer metadata, and more sophisticated platform features.

In parallel, ACM’s publishing program was transitioning from a primarily subscription-based model to one supported by pay-to-publish mechanisms, including ACM Open agreements and author-paid article processing charges. This shift was necessary to support open access at scale, but it also required careful attention to long-term financial sustainability and pricing.

A particularly important tension emerged around institutions that rely heavily on the Digital Library but publish little or not at all in ACM venues. Thousands of institutions worldwide fall into this category. For these institutions, “read and publish” agreements provide limited incentive or perceived value: Because they publish infrequently, they derive little benefit from publication-based arrangements, even though they make extensive use of the Digital Library for research, education, and professional practice.

Maintaining and improving the Digital Library infrastructure requires sustained, ongoing investment in platform development, infrastructure, and data quality. The central challenge, therefore, was not simply how to make content open, but how to do so while preserving quality, enabling innovation, and distributing costs fairly across a global and heterogeneous community.

Taken together, these considerations led ACM to pursue a model that separates open access to the scholarly record from additional platform services, resulting in the introduction of the Basic and Premium service model.

From Open Access to Basic and Premium Digital Library Editions

Basic: Open access as the foundation.  Basic establishes open access as the foundation of the Digital Library. It ensures ACM content can be read, discovered, and used by anyone, anywhere, regardless of affiliation or ability to pay.

The Basic Edition experience is intended to provide:

  • Open access to the scholarly record, including articles and associated materials such as supplemental content, data, software, and videos where available

  • Core discovery and navigation functionality

  • Clear licensing and reuse information

  • Article-level usage and citation indicators to support basic assessment and discovery

  • A consistent experience for readers worldwide

In this sense, Basic represents a commitment to access, equity, and broad dissemination of computing research.

Premium: Sustaining and extending value.  The Premium Edition builds on this foundation by offering enhanced features and services that support more advanced research and address institutional and analytical needs. These enhanced features include tools for deeper discovery, personalization, analytics, and integration that extend beyond basic access to content.

Equally important, Premium provides a subscription option for institutions and individuals who publish infrequently or not at all with ACM. At the same time, the more than 3,100 institutions participating in ACM Open receive Premium as part of their agreement. As a result, the vast majority of institutions and individuals that previously had access to enhanced Digital Library features continue to do so under the new model. Premium helps distribute the cost of platform investment more broadly, supporting sustainability while helping keep publishing-related fees as low as possible.

Community Engagement in the Design

From the outset, ACM sought to involve the community in shaping Basic and Premium models. That engagement took multiple forms, each serving a different purpose.

Focused workshops with librarians, authors, and users enabled detailed, candid discussions about discovery, metrics, sustainability, and perceived value. These smaller forums proved especially effective at surfacing nuanced concerns and practical considerations that would have been difficult to identify through surveys alone.

To deepen feedback during development, the Digital Library Board formed a dedicated Basic/Premium Working Group composed of volunteer board members and staff. This group reviewed feature sets, design mock-ups, and beta functionality, and provided iterative guidance throughout development.

Together, these efforts informed the initial design of the model and highlighted areas where clarity and iteration would be especially important.

Community Reaction

The introduction of Basic and Premium followed a staged rollout rather than a single cutover. A soft launch in mid-December preceded the official launch on Jan. 1, 2026, allowing ACM to observe early reactions, identify areas of confusion as well as emerging concerns, and make targeted adjustments before the new model formally took effect.

That approach was extremely valuable. Feedback received during the soft launch informed refinements to messaging, feature descriptions, and presentation, helping address issues that might otherwise have been amplified at full launch. The official Jan. 1 rollout reflected these early corrections and set a clearer baseline for broader community engagement. Despite this preparation, the official launch of Basic and Premium revealed gaps in shared understanding.

Feedback from across the community highlighted several recurring themes. Many expressed surprise at the introduction of tiered Digital Library services. Some worried that Basic might represent a significant reduction in value compared to the prior version. Others were uncertain about how the two tiers differed in practice, or what the changes signalled for the future of the Digital Library. These concerns were voiced through multiple channels, including town halls, direct correspondence, and community feedback.

In response to the feedback, ACM expanded its listening efforts. These efforts included additional town halls with SIG leadership, direct outreach to librarians and institutional partners, feedback mechanisms within the Digital Library, and continued discussions with volunteer boards and committees.

These conversations were candid and, at times, uncomfortable. They were also essential. In several cases, they surfaced misunderstandings that would not have been obvious otherwise.

Adjustments and Clarifications

Community feedback led directly to several concrete changes, some of which were made quickly.

In response to concerns about visibility and assessment, article-level usage and citation indicators were restored to the Basic experience, and aggregated bibliometric indicators were reintroduced on author pages. These changes reflected the community’s view that such signals are important for understanding reach and impact, even in an open-access environment.

Feedback also highlighted the value of continued, smaller-scale dialogue. In addition to broad town halls, ACM scheduled a series of follow-up discussions with subsets of librarians, authors, and users to explore specific concerns in greater depth and test refinements before wider rollout.

Other adjustments focused on clarity and presentation. Messaging around Basic and Premium was refined, feature descriptions were revisited, and distinctions between open access to content and Digital Library services were made more explicit.

More broadly, this period reinforced the importance of increased transparency around planning and prioritization. As the Digital Library continues to evolve, clearer communication about roadmap direction and decision-making will remain an area of focus.

Experimentation with AI

Another area where community feedback has been especially important is ACM’s experimentation with AI-enabled features in the Digital Library.

As part of the platform evolution, Premium introduced AI-generated short and long summaries intended to help readers more easily discover articles and decide whether they are relevant to their interests. Written in a less technical style than author abstracts, these summaries were designed to support discoverability and encourage more readers to engage with the full articles themselves. While many users found these summaries useful, others raised concerns about accuracy, redundancy with author-written abstracts, and the role of AI-generated content in a scholarly context. Some also expressed discomfort with the use of AI in this setting.

That feedback prompted several immediate adjustments. Disclaimers were added, user feedback mechanisms were introduced, and summary generation workflows were refined. More broadly, it reinforced the need to approach AI features as experiments, subject to iteration and community input, rather than as fixed or definitive representations of scholarly work.

AI experimentation in the Digital Library extends beyond summaries, encompassing a range of discovery and navigation capabilities across articles and conferences. Planned and exploratory efforts include improved integrated search, recommendation capabilities, article-level interaction tools, and conference-oriented services such as personalized navigation, scheduling support, and exploratory access across the full history of a conference. These features are intended to help users navigate an increasingly large and complex corpus, not to replace the underlying scholarly record or the role of authors themselves.

To help guide this work, ACM’s President established the ACM Presidential Task Force on the Use of AI, which is developing principles and guidance for responsible AI use across ACM activities. This task force provides an important governance framework that complements platform-level experimentation.

Within the Digital Library, AI features will continue to be introduced cautiously, evaluated openly, and refined based on feedback. The goal is to explore where AI can meaningfully improve discovery and understanding, while remaining transparent about its limitations and respectful of the scholarly community’s values.

What This Means for Authors

Authors have a distinct relationship with the Digital Library. While all authors are also readers, the Digital Library serves as the authoritative long-term record of their scholarly contributions and professional identity.

The transition to open access is, at its core, an author-centred change. By removing barriers to reading and sharing, it increases the visibility, reach, and potential impact of ACM publications and their authors. For many authors, it also aligns ACM’s publication programs more closely with funder and institutional open-access requirements.

At the same time, community feedback made clear that access alone is not sufficient. Authors care deeply about how their work is presented, discovered, and contextualized over time. Common questions focused on how articles reach broader audiences, how publication records and archives are represented across years and venues, and which Digital Library features are available to authors independent of their institution’s access model.

In response, ongoing development increasingly treats the author as a first-class role within the Digital Library. This shift includes continued investment in author profile pages, clearer presentation of publication records and archives, improved linking across venues and versions, and better signalling of open-access status and reuse rights.

Looking ahead, we see clear opportunities for the Digital Library to better reflect the author’s perspective. These include views that highlight authorship, publication records, and archives, and support an author’s ongoing relationship with their work, alongside existing reader and institutional views.

Governance and Oversight

Throughout this transition, the Digital Library operated under the oversight of the ACM Digital Library Board, which reports into ACM’s broader governance structure. The Digital Library Board includes volunteer representation from across the community and plays a central role in stewarding the platform’s evolution, guiding strategic direction, reviewing major changes, and ensuring that community perspectives inform decision making.

The scope and impact of recent changes have also prompted reflection on governance itself. As the Digital Library increasingly sits at the intersection of publishing, access, technology, and policy, it is important that its governance continues to evolve in parallel. This includes ensuring appropriate representation and coordination across ACM’s major boards and stakeholder groups, commensurate with the Digital Library’s central role in ACM’s mission.

Looking Ahead

The transition to open access, Basic, and Premium is not a completed project—it is an evolving framework.

Future work includes continued refinement of both tiers based on usage and feedback, clearer articulation of value for different communities, and ongoing evaluation of sustainability and equity. Regular opportunities for community input will remain central to this process.

Lessons Learned

Several lessons stand out from this transition, which will inform how ACM approaches future platform and policy changes.

  • Transparency must begin early and continue often, especially during periods of change.

  • Engagement must be both broad and deep, combining large-scale input with focused discussion.

  • Open access changes expectations, not just licensing.

  • Staged rollouts, including soft launches, create space to learn, adjust, and reduce unnecessary friction at full release.

  • Experimentation, particularly with emerging technologies such as AI, requires clear framing, visible safeguards, and ongoing community dialogue.

  • Governance and communication must evolve alongside the platforms they oversee.

  • The ACM community has an essential role in shaping expectations, policy, and the strategic direction of the Digital Library.

Conclusion

At its core, the ACM Digital Library exists to serve its community. The transition to open access and the introduction of Basic and Premium were driven by that mission, even when the path forward proved complex.

We are grateful for the engagement, critique, and collaboration of authors, readers, librarians, volunteers, and staff. That engagement has strengthened the Digital Library and helped align it more closely with the values it represents.

As the platform continues to evolve, including careful experimentation with new technologies, the work will remain guided by transparency, responsibility, and sustained dialogue with the community it serves.
item.dc:creator: Wayne Graves

item.title: General Game Playing
item.dc:creator: Michael Genesereth
item.description: General game playing, while interesting in its own right, serves as a lab for practical applications in business and law, science and engineering.
item.content:encoded:
Games of strategy, such as chess and checkers, couple intellectual activity with competition. We can exercise and improve our intellectual skills by playing such games. The competition adds excitement and allows us to compare our skills to those of others. The same motivation accounts for interest in computer game playing as a testbed for artificial intelligence (AI). The idea is that programs that think better should be able to win more games, so we can use game playing as an evaluation technique for intelligent systems.

Unfortunately, building programs to play specific games has limited value in this regard. To begin with, specialized game players have a very narrow focus. They can be good at one game but not another. Deep Blue2 may have beaten the world chess champion, but it has no clue how to play checkers. A second, more subtle problem with specialized game-playing systems is they do only part of the work. Most of the interesting analysis and design is done in advance by their programmers. The systems themselves might as well be tele-operated.

All is not lost. The idea of game playing can be used to good effect to inspire and evaluate good work in Artificial Intelligence, but it requires moving more of the design work to the computer itself. This can be done by focusing attention on general game playing.

General game players are systems that accept descriptions of arbitrary games at runtime and that use such descriptions to play those games effectively without human intervention. In other words, they do not know the rules until the games start.

Unlike specialized game players, such as Deep Blue2 and Stockfish, general game players must be able to play different kinds of games. They must be able to play simple games (like tic-tac-toe) and complex games (like chess), games with differing numbers of players, cooperative games and competitive games, games with or without communication among the players, and so forth. Importantly, they must be able to play games they have never seen before, including games no one has seen before.

Importantly, general game players cannot rely on algorithms designed in advance for specific games as in the case of specialized game players. General-game-playing (GGP) expertise depends on intelligence on the part of the game player and not just intelligence of the programmer of the game player.

General game playing underscores the importance of knowledge representation, reasoning, and rational decision making in a world increasingly focused on machine learning and large language models. It is worth noting that, despite important and impressive game-related developments in these latter areas (e.g. Bannerjee et al.,1 Hausknecht et al.,11 Levine,16 and Silver.25), no machine learning program has ever won an official GGP competition due to time limits on game play, and it is unlikely that systems based on large language models (LLMs) would perform any better, due to limits on time and space and due to “obfuscation” (wherein words in game descriptions are replaced by nonsense words). By contrast, undergraduates with minimal training in knowledge representation and reasoning methods are able to create GGP programs running on laptops that excel in such competitions and frequently beat humans.

Recent History

The idea of general problem solving dates from the earliest days of AI. In 1958, Newell and Simon proposed a general problem solver19 that worked by applying general, task-independent problem-solving processes to descriptions of domain-specific tasks. The first explicit mention of GGP appeared in a subsequent paper by Jacques Pitrat21 in 1968. A related notion was described by Barney Pell,20 who further developed the concept in his 1993 doctoral thesis. In the early 2000s, Stanford University researchers enlarged the concept from chess-like games to arbitrary discrete dynamic systems, thus broadening the concept and making it applicable beyond the world of recreational games.

To promote work on GGP, in 2005 the Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI) established the International General Game Playing Competition,7,8 an annual contest to determine the best automated general game players in the world. The following table shows the winners of the first dozen competitions.

In addition to pitting automated players against each other, the competition frequently included a man-machine demonstration match between the competition winner and a human player(s). While the human player won the first of these demonstrations, the computer won all subsequent competitions. For example, in 2012, CadiaPlayer, in addition to defeating the other automated competitors, defeated the human race, represented by Chris Welty, one of the developers of Watson, the IBM computer that defeated the best players on the American game show Jeopardy! (As a consolation prize, the human was awarded two bottles of Scotch to ease his disappointment at letting down the human race.)

The International GGP Competition was suspended after 2016. However, by then, the competition had served its primary purpose—it led to workshops on GGP at multiple international conferences and the publication of numerous research papers on GGP and its applications. Today, regional competitions continue to be run, and there are rumors that the international competition will resume before long, albeit in a slightly different format.

Today, there are GGP sites in multiple countries around the world. The Gamemaster site contains a specification of the game description language, games encoded in this language, and software for building players and running matches.

Game Playing

Despite the variety of games treated in GGP, all games share a common abstract structure. Each game takes place in an environment with finitely many states, with one distinguished initial state and one or more terminal states. In addition, each game has a fixed, finite number of players; each player has finitely many possible actions in any game state, and each state has an associated goal value for each player.

Given this common structure, we can think of a game as a state graph, like the one shown in Figure 2. In this case, we have a game with one player, with eight states (named s1, … , s8). The arcs in this graph capture the transition function for the game. For example, if the game is in state s1 and a player performs action a, the game will move to state s2. If the game is in state s1 and a player performs action b, the game will move to state s5. Each game has just one initial state, in this case s1. However, there can be any number of terminal states, in this case s4 and s8. The numbers associated with each state indicate the utilities of those states for the player. Players earn those scores only in terminal states. However, they are provided for all states and in some games indicate incremental progress.

We can extend this model to accommodate games with multiple players with two modifications.

  1. We annotate each state with rewards for all players in the game. (The rewards can be the same or different for different players.)

  2. For each state, we specify which player is in control, that is, whose turn it is to play. Turns need not strictly alternate; in some cases, a single player may get several turns in a row. (This model can be extended to games with simultaneous moves, but for the sake of simplicity, we avoid that complexity in what follows.)

Since all the games we are considering are finite, it is possible, in principle, to describe such games in the form of state graphs. Unfortunately, such explicit representations are not practical in all cases. Even though the numbers of states and actions are finite, these sets can be extremely large; and the corresponding graphs can be larger still. For example, in chess, there are more than 1040 possible states.

In the vast majority of games, states and actions have composite structure that allows us to define a large number of states and actions in terms of a smaller number of more fundamental entities. In chess, for example, states are not monolithic; they can be conceptualized in terms of pieces, squares, rows, columns, diagonals, and so forth. By exploiting this structure, it is possible to encode games in a form that is more compact than direct representation.

The first step in solving this problem is to conceptualize states as datasets (as sets of facts that are true in those states) and to conceptualize actions as operations applied to arguments, as suggested by the graph in Figure 3.

Given a “structured” state graph of this sort, the second step is to encode game rules in terms of these datasets and actions. In GGP, the most popular game description language is GDL (for Game Description Language).17 Rules in GDL are written in Epilog,9 a dynamic logic programming language based on Prolog. See below for the rules of tic-tac-toe as written in GDL.


role(x)
role(o)
 
init(cell(1,1,b))
init(cell(1,2,b))
init(cell(1,3,b))
init(cell(2,1,b))
init(cell(2,2,b))
init(cell(2,3,b))
init(cell(3,1,b))
init(cell(3,2,b))
init(cell(3,3,b))
init(control(x))
 
legal(mark(M,N)) :- cell(M,N,b)
 
mark(M,N) :: control(R)  ==> cell(M,N,R) & ~cell(M,N,b)
mark(M,N) :: control(x) ==> ~control(x) & control(o)
mark(M,N) :: control(o) ==> ~control(o) & control(x)
 
line(Z) :- row(M,Z)
line(Z) :- column(M,Z)
line(Z) :- diagonal(Z)
row(M,X) :- cell(M,1,X) & cell(M,2,X) & cell(M,3,X)
column(N,X) :- cell(1,N,X) & cell(2,N,X) & cell(3,N,X)
diagonal(X) :- cell(1,1,X) & cell(2,2,X) & cell(3,3,X)
diagonal(X) :- cell(1,3,X) & cell(2,2,X) & cell(3,1,X)
 
goal(x,100) :- line(x) & ~line(o)
goal(x,50) :- ~line(x) & ~line(o)
goal(x,0) :- ~line(x) & line(o)
goal(o,100) :- ~line(x) & line(o)
goal(o,50) :- ~line(x) & ~line(o)
goal(o,0) :- line(x) & ~line(o)
 
terminal :- line(x)
terminal :- line(o)
terminal :- ~open
 
open :- true(cell(M,N,b))

There are two players: x and o. In the initial state, all cells are blank (represented here by the symbol b), and the x player has control. It is legal for the player in control to mark a cell if that cell is blank. (In GDL, symbols that begin with capital letters are variables, while symbols that begin with lower case letters are constants. The :- operator is read as “if”—the expression to its left is true if the expressions that follow it are true.) If the player in control marks a cell, that cell contains the player’s mark in the next state and the b is removed. Also, control alternates on each play. (The :: operator relates the action mentioned to its left as a transition rule that applies whenever the conditions before the ==> are true and produces a state in which the conditions to its right are true.) A line is a row of marks, a column of marks, or a diagonal. The x player / o player receives 100 points in any state with a line of x marks / o marks, it receives 50 points in any state with no lines, and it gets 0 points in any state with a line of o marks / x marks. Finally, a game terminates whenever there is a line of player marks or if the game is no longer open, that is, there are no blank cells.

The main thing to note about this example is that one page of rules fully describes a game of thousands of states. That is a significant savings over the state graph for tic-tac-toe (which contains more than 5,000 states). The compression in more complex games can be even more dramatic. For example, it is possible to describe the rules of chess in just four pages of rules like the ones above.

Interestingly, to prevent programmers from building in specialized capabilities for specific words in game descriptions, it is common in general game playing to obfuscate descriptions. All words are consistently replaced by nonsense words. The only exceptions are variables and game-independent constants, such as role, legal, terminal.

Game Playing

Having a formal description of a game is one thing; being able to use that description to play the game effectively is something else entirely. The player must be able to compute the initial state of the game. It must be able to compute which moves are legal in every state. It must be able to determine the state resulting from particular moves. It must be able to compute the value of each state for each player. And it must be able to determine whether any given state is terminal.

From a description such as this, a general game player can reconstruct a game tree. See the partial game tree shown in Figure 4. The player starts with the initial state, computes the legal moves in that state, and for each move deduces the next state. This is done repeatedly to expand the tree until a terminal state is reached on each branch. Given a game-tree, a player can use minimax22 or an equivalent technique to determine its best possible move, and it can use sophisticated search techniques, such as alpha-beta pruning, to save work.14

Unfortunately, it is not always possible to search to the end of the game tree. In tic-tac-toe, there are thousands of states. This is large but manageable. In chess, there are more than 1040 states.24 A state space of this size, being finite, is fully searchable in principle but not in practice. Moreover, the time limit on moves in most games means players must select actions without knowing with certainty whether they are the best or even good moves to make.

The alternative is to do incomplete search, on each move expanding the game tree as much as possible within the available time and then making a choice based on the estimated values of non-terminal states. In traditional game playing, where the rules are known in advance, the programmer can invent game-specific evaluation functions to help in this regard. For example, in chess, we know that states with higher piece count and greater board control are better than ones with less material or lower control. Unfortunately, it is not possible for a GGP programmer to invent such game-specific rules in advance, as the game’s rules are not known until the game begins. The program must evaluate states for itself. Doing this effectively is the key to effective general game playing.

The approach used in early GGP programs was to develop game-independent heuristics,3,13,15,23 for example, proximity to a goal state, player mobility, and opponent restriction. Consider mobility. Proponents argue that, all other things being equal, it is better to move to a state that affords the player greater mobility and offers more possible actions than to be boxed into a corner. Symmetrically, proponents of mobility argue that it is good to minimize the mobility of one’s opponents. All of these heuristics have been shown to be effective in some games. Unfortunately, they are only heuristics. They frequently fail, sometimes with comical consequences.

The final match of GGP-06 is an example. The game was cylinder checkers, that is, checkers played on a cylinder. In this game, as in ordinary checkers, a player is permitted to move one of their ordinary pieces (pieces that are not kings) one square forward on each turn. In the position shown in Figure 5, red is moving from top to bottom and black is moving from bottom to top. If a piece is blocked by an opponent’s player, he can “jump” that player if there is an empty square on the other side. Moreover, the player must make such a jump if one is available. The objective of the game is to take all or as many of the opponent’s pieces as possible while preserving one’s own pieces. Here is a snapshot of the game. It is red’s turn to play. What should it do? And what do you think it did?

Here’s a hint. The player in this case was Cluneplayer;3 and it had decided, for some reason or other, that limiting the opponent’s mobility was a good heuristic. If it were to move the rearmost piece, black would have multiple moves. However, if it were to move the piece in front, black would be forced to capture its piece. In other words, it would have at most one move. Clearly, moving the forward piece minimizes the opponent’s mobility, so that is what Cluneplayer did. Actually, the whole match played out this way, with red giving black captures at every opportunity. It was sad to watch but also a little comical. The moral is that, while non-guaranteed heuristics are sometimes useful, they are not always useful.

Monte Carlo Search

The second generation of GGP programs used a different approach to evaluating non-terminal states, notably Monte Carlo Search and Monte Carlo Tree Search.

In Monte Carlo Search (MCS), the player expands the tree a few levels. Then, rather than using a local heuristic to evaluate a state, it makes some probes from that state to the end of the game by selecting random moves for all players. It sums up the total rewards for all such probes and divides by the number of probes to obtain an estimated utility for that state. It can then use these expected utilities in comparing states and selecting actions.

Monte Carlo Tree Search6 improves on this by replacing the random choice with more intelligent selections. The result of introducing such techniques was dramatic. Suddenly, automated general game players began to perform at a high level. Using MCTS, Cadioplayer won the competition three times.

The use of statistical techniques changed the character of general game players from curiosities to programs capable of serious play. Virtually every general game playing program today includes some variant of MCS or MCTS. Unfortunately, statistical search alone has weaknesses, notably on games with complex descriptions requiring a lot of computation time, and on games where there are few goal states; and, consequently, it must be combined with other methods to be successful.

Metagaming

Whereas the GGP techniques described above concentrated on game tree search performed at runtime, subsequent work was focused on offline processing of game descriptions. Examples include things like factoring games into independent subgames, reformulating game descriptions, and automatic programming. Often it is the case that this sort of processing more than pays for itself. In such cases, players can expend a little time up front and gain a lot in processing time later.

This is really what programmers do when building specialized game players, and now we are building game players that can do these things for themselves. And this is what GGP was intended to stress from its very beginnings.

One example of offline analysis is game decomposition, also called factoring.4,10 Consider the game of Hodgepodge pictured in Figure 7. Hodgepodge is actually two games glued together. Here we show chess and Othello, but it could be any two games. One move in a joint game of Hodgepodge corresponds to one move in each of the two constituent games. Winning requires winning at least one of the two games while not losing the other.

What makes Hodgepodge interesting is that it is factorable, that is it can be divided into two independent games. Realizing this can have dramatic benefit. To see this, consider the size of the game tree for Hodgepodge. Suppose that one game tree has branching a and the other has branching factor b. Then the branching factor of the joint game is a times b, and the size of the fringe of the game tree at level n is (a*b)ˆn. However, the two games are independent. Moving in one subgame does not affect the state of the other subgame. So, the player really should be searching two smaller game trees, one with branching factor a and the other with branching factor b. In this way, at depth n, there would be only aˆn+bˆn states. This is a huge decrease in the size of the search space. Luckily, in many cases, it is possible to discover such factors in time proportional to the size of the game description.

The following table illustrates the computational benefits of factoring on three highly factorable games: multiple buttons and lights, multiple switches, and multiple tic-tac-toe. The first column in the table identifies the game, the second column indicates the depth to which the tree is searched, the third column lists the number of milliseconds to search the game tree to that depth without factoring, and the last column shows the number of milliseconds after factoring.

Factoring is just one example of game reformulation. There are many others. For example, it is sometimes possible to find symmetries in games that cut down on search space. In some games, there are bottlenecks that allow for a different type of factoring. Consider, for example, a game made up of one or more subgames in which it is necessary to win one game before moving onto a second game. In such a case, there is no need to search to a terminal state in the overall game; it is sufficient to limit search to termination in the current subgame. These examples are extreme cases, but there are many simpler everyday examples of finding structure of this sort that can help in curtailing search.

The trick in metagaming is to analyze and/or reformulate a game description without expanding the entire game tree. The interesting thing about general game playing is that sometimes the cost of analysis is proportional to the size of the description rather than the size of the game tree, as in the example mentioned above. In such cases, players can expend a little time and gain a lot in search savings.

Of course, factoring games and finding transformations such as these requires a lot of algorithmic expertise. What we really need is Donald Knuth in a box. Or maybe Corman and Leiserson. And, of course, since we are talking about games, we should have expertise at game tree search. So we had better cram Jonathan Schaeffer into our box as well.

An important point here is that GGP is not just about game tree search; to an even greater extent, it is about game descriptions and their use, and ultimately about automatic programming.

Conclusion

General game playing is an interesting application in its own right. It is intellectually engaging and more than a little fun. But it is much more than that. It serves as a laboratory for practical applications, in business and law, science and engineering. In fact, some of the games used in competitions are drawn from such areas. More fundamentally, it provides a theoretical framework for defining rationality in a way that takes into account problem representation, incompleteness of information, and resource bounds. The upshot is that it raises questions about the nature of intelligence and serves as a laboratory in which to evaluate competing approaches to intelligence.

General game playing is a setting within which AI is the essential technology. It concentrates attention on the notion of runnable specifications. Building systems of this sort dates from the early years of AI.

It was in 1958 that John McCarthy invented the concept of the “advice taker.”18 The idea was simple. He wanted a machine that he could program by description. He would describe the intended environment and the desired goal, and the machine would use that information to determine its behavior. There would be no programming in the traditional sense. McCarthy presented his concept in a paper that has become a classic in the field of AI:

“The main advantage we expect the advice taker to have is that its behavior will be improvable merely by making statements to it, telling it about its environment and what is wanted from it. To make these statements will require little, if any, knowledge of the program or the previous knowledge of the advice taker.”

John McCarthy

An ambitious goal! But that was a time of high hopes and grand ambitions. The idea caught the imaginations of numerous subsequent researchers—notably Bob Kowalski, the high priest of logic programming, and Ed Feigenbaum, the inventor of knowledge engineering. In a paper written in 1974, Feigenbaum gave his most forceful statement of McCarthy’s ideal:5

“The potential use of computers by people to accomplish tasks can be ‘one-dimensionalized’ into a spectrum representing the nature of the instruction that must be given the computer to do its job. Call it the what-to-how spectrum. At one extreme of the spectrum, the user supplies his intelligence to instruct the machine with precision exactly how to do his job step-by-step … At the other end of the spectrum is the user with his real problem … He aspires to communicate what he wants done … without having to lay out in detail all necessary subgoals for adequate performance.”

Ed Feigenbaum

Some have argued that the way to achieve intelligent behavior is through specialization. That may work so long as the assumptions one makes in building such systems are true. For general intelligence, however, general intellectual capabilities are needed, and such systems should be capable of performing well in a wide variety of tasks. To quote the words of Robert Heinlein:12

“A human being should be able to change a diaper, plan an invasion, butcher a hog, conn a ship, design a building, write a sonnet, balance accounts, build a wall, set a bone, comfort the dying, take orders, give orders, cooperate, act alone, solve equations, analyze a new problem, pitch manure, program a computer, cook a tasty meal, fight efficiently, die gallantly. Specialization is for insects.”

Robert Heinlein

item.title: In Memoriam: David J. Farber
item.dc:creator: Simson L. Garfinkel and Eugene H. Spafford
item.description: The 'Grandfather of the Internet' made networking his life's work.
item.content:encoded:
Networking was David J. Farber’s life’s work. Farber helped design the first electronic switching system (ESS-1) during his 11-year career at Bell Laboratories (1956-1967). Later, as an academic who held faculty appointments at five universities, the moderator of the highly influential “Interesting People” mailing list that chronicled the intersection of technology and policy on the early Internet, and the chief technologist of the U.S. Federal Communications Commission (FCC) from 2000-2001, he specialized in networking people.

Farber died suddenly at his home in Roppongi, Tokyo, on Feb. 7, 2026, at the age of 91.

Farber graduated from Stevens Institute of Technology in Hoboken, NJ, USA, with a bachelor’s degree in electrical engineering in 1956 and joined the technical staff of the Electronic Switching Engineering Systems Department at Bell Telephone Laboratories, where he worked on the ESS-1 team. His particular emphasis was on software structure and software support infrastructure.

In 1961, Farber completed a master’s in math from Stevens; in 1963, he completed the Communication Development Program at Bell Labs, which he noted was equivalent to a master’s degree in electrical engineering. In 1965, he was promoted to supervisor, responsible for the Holmdel Computer Center, a key player in the design and implementation of the Multics operating system.

Farber joined the technical staff of the RAND Corporation in 1967 to work on Paul Baran’s vision of communications systems that could survive a nuclear war. This project gave birth to packet networking and the Internet. He joined the technical staff of Scientific Data Systems (SDS) in 1969 to continue the work, while simultaneously teaching at the University of California, Irvine, in the evenings.

Xerox acquired SDS in 1969. Unhappy with the company’s stewardship, Farber decided to go full-time at UCI.

“Because of my industrial experience and the fact that, pragmatically, I never got around to getting a doctorate, I had an acting associate professor appointment, so I had two years to do something,” Farber told an interviewer in his 1988 oral history.3

Farber submitted a proposal to the NSF to build a decentralized computer system with three minicomputers connected via a token-ring local area network; it was funded at $250,000 per year ($2.1M today), securing Farber his first tenured faculty position. Among his Ph.D. students were Internet pioneers Jonathan Postel (creator of the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority), Marshall Rose (inventor of the Simple Network Management Protocol), and Paul Mockapetris (inventor of the Domain Name System). His students’ work led Farber to be called the ‘Grandfather of the Internet.’

In 1975, DARPA directed MIT’s Laboratory for Computer Science to adopt Farber’s token ring technology as the basis for its development. Ultimately, LCS adopted Ethernet, an alternative technology, but IBM adopted token ring and used it throughout the 1990s.

In 1977, Farber joined the University of Delaware as a professor of electrical engineering and professor of computer science. There, he led the development of the Multichannel Memorandum Distribution Facility (MMDF), an email system widely used on Unix systems for nearly three decades.

In 1985, the National Science Foundation created NSFNET, a civilian IP-based network to link U.S. universities that had previously been connected only by ARPANET and non-IP networks such as BITNET and the store-and-forward UUCP network. NSF’s director, Erich Bloch, told Farber he was having trouble keeping up with all the news; Farber responded by forwarding to Bloch timely, on-topic email messages. Soon, Bloch suggested adding a few of his colleagues at IBM as recipients of the messages. As the recipient list grew, Farber named his list “Interesting People.” At its peak, the list had more than 25,000 recipients. The online archives, with 37,154 messages from May 1993 to February 2019, offered a curated view of the growth of the commercial Internet and the World Wide Web, as well as the range of resultant technical, political, and social issues.

“Supercomputing is on the verge of becoming a utility,” Farber told John Markoff of The New York Times in 1992. “You may want more computing power for a very short period of time. In the future, without even asking, your computer will reach out over high-speed computing networks to get that power.” We live in that world today, although for most people, the computer that seamlessly integrates with the cloud-based supercomputing utilities is the smartphone in their hands.

“It was David who first alerted me to the societal significance of computer networks and to distributed computing more generally,” said Markoff, who wrote for the paper from 1988 until 2016. “As I became more interested in high-performance computing and high-speed networks, David was one of my most valued sources.”

Farber moved to the University of Pennsylvania in 1988, where he worked on ultra-high-speed networking, examining the relationship between processor interconnect, protocols, and software. He eventually added appointments as professor of business and public policy at the University’s Wharton School of Business, and as a faculty associate at its Annenberg School of Communications.

“Perhaps the single most important thing he taught me, that I have never forgotten, is to let your graduate students be independent, let them explore widely, never stifle their creativity and they’ll become truly capable researchers,” said University of Pennsylvania professor Jonathan M. Smith, who joined Penn as an assistant professor in 1989.

“With respect to the Gigabit Testbeds, Dave discovered that researchers at Bellcore Bell Communications Research . . . had been working on (and had succeeded at building) a novel data switch fabric that could interconnect 100Mb/s Asynchronous Transfer Mode links,” wrote Smith in an email. At the time, most computer networks ran at 10 Mbps. “Dave quickly grasped the implications” of the work at Bellcore, and arranged for people and funding from multiple sources; by 1993, the team had created the world’s first gigabit network from Penn to Bellcore in New Jersey.1

Farber joined the Board of Trustees of his alma mater, Stevens Institute of Technology, in 1997, and was awarded an honorary doctorate by the institution in 1999. Farber took a leave of absence from UPenn in June 2000 to serve a one-year appointment as chief technologist at the FCC.

“We were on the Task Force on National Security together, and I subscribed to his labor-of-love tech news roundup,” said Stewart Baker, the first assistant secretary for policy at the U.S. Department of Homeland Security and former general counsel of the National Security Agency. “He had the usual views of academia on political and tech issues but was happy to talk to people with very different views. He was endlessly open to new information and a pleasure to know.”

Farber retired from UPenn in 2003 and immediately joined Carnegie Mellon University as a Distinguished Career Professor of Computer Science and Public Policy in the School of Computer Science, with a secondary appointment in the Heinz School of Public Policy and the Engineering Public Policy Group.

Over the course of his career, Farber became increasingly concerned about the use of technology to control individuals, the fragility of the Internet he helped create, and the impact of increasingly powerful technologies on individuals and society at large.

Author Cory Doctorow recalled that after Farber left the FCC, the Interesting People mailing list was used to organize opposition to the Broadcast Flag, a proposal that would have imposed restrictions on computers for handling copyrighted video. Doctorow had a “strategy call” with Farber, in which Farber recommended: “Don’t call the people you’re standing up for ‘users’ or ‘consumers.’ Call them ‘computer owners’ and ‘citizens.’ Make sure the person you’re talking to understands that this is about your right, as a member of society, to control how your own computer works, and whether it serves you.”

In 2006, Farber was one of 17 computer scientists to sign an amici curiae brief to the U.S. Supreme Court in the case of MGM Studios vs. Grokster.4 In 2016, Farber said that the Internet must be secure but that “it was never built to be securable. . . . We’re sitting here with critical resources on top of systems we can’t protect. . . . And I think it’s our duty as people who want a healthy Internet to start paying attention to that.”2

Farber had a deep love of Japan and had visited the country dozens of times. Following the death of his wife of 45 years, Gloria (G.G.), he moved to Japan in 2018 to become a Distinguished Professor at Keio University, in Tokyo.

“Dave’s relationship with Japan began in 1973, when Keio’s Professor Hideo Aiso made an extended visit to Dave’s group at UC Irvine,” said Jun Murai, professor and dean of the faculty at Keio, who is frequently called “the father of the Internet in Japan.”

“Dave’s influence on networking and distributed systems research in Japan is hard to overstate, and he was very proud to have given the first public lecture at Keio’s Shonan Fujisawa Campus when it opened in 1990,” Murai continued. In April 2018, Farber and Murai co-founded the Cyber Civilization Research Center at Keio University to study the societal, economic, and technological impacts of digital transformation.

Farber loved teaching and continued to teach until his final class on January 22, 2026, 16 days before his death. He served on the board of directors of the Electronic Frontier Foundation, the Board of Trustees of the Internet Society, the advisory boards of Electronic Privacy Information Center and the Center for Democracy and Technology, and was a member of the President’s Information Technology Advisory Committee (PITAC, formerly the Presidential Advisory Committee on High-Performance Computing and Communications, Information Technology, and the Next Generation Internet). He was a fellow of the ACM, the IEEE, and AAAS. He received the 1995 SIGCOMM Award for his lifelong contributions to computer communications, and in 1997, the John Scott Award for Contributions to Humanity.

Farber is survived by his son, Manny Farber; daughter-in-law, Mei Xu; daughter-in-law, Carol Hagan, widow of his son Joseph M. Farber; and grandsons, Nate Farber and Sam Farber.
item.dc:creator: Eugene H. Spafford

item.title: When Seeing Isn’t Believing
item.dc:creator: Alex Wright
item.description: The quest to understand how human memory works has taken on new urgency in the age of AI as researchers discover how fragile, and easily manipulated, recollections can be.
item.content:encoded:
When a Reddit user known as “ibreakphotos” first snapped a picture of the Moon with his new Samsung Galaxy S20 Ultra phone, he was startled by the result: a razor-sharp image of the lunar surface, with every crater and shadow etched in sharp relief.

There was just one problem—the picture wasn’t exactly real.

“Something seemed off,” he recalled. As a hobbyist photographer with a basic grasp of optics, he found the level of detail in the image suspicious. Out of curiosity, he snapped another photo—this time of an intentionally blurred image of the moon displayed on his computer monitor. The result: another magically detailed image. He concluded there was only one possible explanation: “It’s AI.”

When he posted his findings to Reddit, the results went viral. “I didn’t expect it to blow up as much as it did,” he said, but his experiment seems to have struck a nerve. As AI becomes increasingly embedded in the cameras, apps, and assistants that mediate our lived experiences—retouching our photos, summarizing our conversations, and quietly editing the digital records of our lives—it is also subtly altering the contents of our memories.

The growing influence of AI in shaping human recollection is capturing the attention of psychologists and computer scientists alike. Recent experiments suggest that generative systems don’t just alter the contents of digital files; they can also fundamentally change what people think they have seen—with implications stretching from relatively mundane use cases like vacation photos to the high-stakes realms of politics, finance, and criminal justice.

What exactly is memory, anyway? Philosophers from Buddha to Plato to William James have grappled with that question. Yet in the age of AI, the age-old quest to understand how human memory works has taken on new urgency, as researchers discover just how fragile—and easily manipulated—our recollections can be.

“Many people think that memory works like a recording device: you record the event and then you play it back later,” said University of California, Irvine, psychologist Elizabeth Loftus. However, the truth is far more complex. “We’re often taking bits and pieces from different times and places, and we bring it together and construct the memory.”

Loftus’ work has shown that people can be persuaded through suggestive interviewing—in some cases, to the point where they recall events that never actually happened.

Harvard psychologist Daniel Schachter has gone further, cataloging the seven “sins” of memory— transience, absentmindedness, blocking, misattribution, suggestibility, bias, and persistence—innate human foibles that make all of us unreliable narrators of our own lives.

In other words, memory works less like a camera than a collage—selected, assembled, and subject to interpretation. In a world awash in AI-generated content, opportunities are multiplying for bad actors to hijack the human memory-making apparatus.

Recent studies are bringing these risks into stark relief. At the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) Media Lab, Pat Pataranutaporn and colleagues discovered that AI-generated images and videos can not only blur the lines between real and fake, they can also give people extraordinary levels of confidence in false memories. The team showed 200 participants a set of original photos and videos, then replaced some with versions altered by generative AI. Later, participants who had been exposed to the AI-enhanced versions recalled false details with the same level of conviction as those who had seen the originals. The effect was strongest with hybrid combinations of video and images, which made participants even more certain of false memories compared to factually correct ones.

A separate study by Samantha Chan of Singapore’s Nanyang Technological University explored how generative AI chatbots can also be used for deceptive ends. In simulated interviews, participants interacting with a chatbot programmed to add false details to a conversation were more than three times as likely to recall falsehoods as those prompted with a static questionnaire. The study underscores that the danger lies not merely in disseminating falsehoods, but in the persuasive, adaptive style of generative dialogue.

“The chatbot was intentionally designed to agree with the users,” said Chan.
