She believes there could be opportunities to engineer chat interfaces with more critical reasoning baked in; for example, through Socratic-style questioning, or other cues reminding them that these digital artifacts have been edited. “How could we subtly enhance people’s critical thinking when interacting with these systems?”

Not every experiment points towards dystopian outcomes, however. In Barcelona, design researcher Pau Garcia is leading a team exploring how AI-generated photographs might help elderly patients with dementia reconstruct and engage with their own past, in a process known as reminiscence therapy. Their early research shows promise, and they are now testing how AI might help people reconstruct memories lost through traumatic events—for example, refugees who have lost their photo albums. But any attempt to fashion such prosthetic memory aids is inevitably fraught with ethical landmines.

“There is no such thing as benign manipulation,” said Marie Potel-Saville, CEO of Fairpatterns, a think tank devoted to helping companies identify and address dark patterns in AI applications. Ultimately, she believes that users should have maximal control over whether and how AI is used to manipulate information they have created—no matter how well-intentioned the project. “The ethical way is to empower users to make fair and informed choices,” she said, arguing that industry and government need to maximize transparency and disclosure to ensure users understand how any given artifact was created.

These principles are starting to take hold in both industry and regulatory dialogues. In the European Union’s Artificial Intelligence Act, one article specifically prohibits any form of manipulation “below the level of consciousness.” Meanwhile, major AI vendors like OpenAI, Microsoft, Meta, Google, Intel, and Amazon have joined the newly formed Coalition for Content Provenance and Authority (C2PA), which is developing a specification for “Content Credentials”—a kind of digital nutrition label showing the provenance and history of any image, audio, or video file.

As these technical and regulatory frameworks take shape, however, a deeper epistemic challenge still remains. People and machines process information in fundamentally different ways. Whereas humans construct memories out of their own flawed, fragmentary recollections, computers can (at least in principle) record everything indiscriminately. The result is a collision between two very different kinds of recall: one that is subjective and highly constructed, and another that is seemingly empirical and never forgets. But what happens when those two different ways of understanding the world contradict each other—and, complicating matters even further, what if the machines themselves begin to disagree?

Petar Popovski, a communication engineering professor at Denmark’s Aalborg University, has been thinking about precisely that question. He warns of “Rashomon effects” (after the famous Akira Kurosawa film), where multiple devices observing the same event might disagree about its sequence. That problem grows even thornier when humans try to reconcile their own recollections with conflicting machine accounts.

“Humans don’t remember everything they have seen,” Popovski said, “we just remember important details.” But computers are not so selective. As human memories become intertwined with recordings from multiple cameras, sensors, and satellites—each with its own network delays and enhancement algorithms—these discrepancies pose a risk of what he calls “causality violation” or “temporal displacement”: a kind of digital motion sickness.

Computer scientists have long grappled with the issue of sequencing events. In the 1970s, Leslie Lamport’s seminal research showed how distributed systems could maintain consistent event sequences through synchronized timestamps. Yet today’s networks are far messier: a vast, asynchronous array of sensors, relay mechanisms, base stations, satellites, and AI filters. Even small delays can cascade through such a complex ecosystem into large distortions, altering our sense of cause and effect—and creating openings for manipulation by bad actors.

“Imagine there are multiple devices capturing an event in real time, and someone inserts a murder in there,” Popovski said. What happens, in other words, when the machines we rely on as eyewitnesses start to lose their credibility?

Pataranutaporn and his colleagues have proposed the need for “memory hygiene” practices—like watermarks, provenance tracking, and edit disclosures—to preserve context and authenticity in an AI-saturated world. And Popovski’s research on event ordering and causal integrity underscores the importance of timestamping and synchronization in defending against distortions in how events are recorded.

As generative models become woven into everyday sense-making and record-keeping activities, these safeguards will become ever more critical. Ultimately, the dividing line between reality and digital fabrication is no longer a fixed boundary, but an ever-shifting frontier.

In the classic 1966 Philip K. Dick story “We Can Remember It for You Wholesale” (the basis for the movie Total Recall), the protagonist Douglas Quail dreams of taking a trip to Mars, and turns to a company called Rekall Inc. that implants false memories—for a fee. As the story develops, he discovers that his new memories become deeply intertwined with his real ones, with maddening and ultimately devastating results.

What makes the story so unsettling is that Quail’s artificially implanted memories feel every bit as authentic to him as real ones, raising a crucial question: in an age when our recollections can be manipulated through machine learning, will human memory become just another medium—or will we rediscover the timeless truth that memory has always been, more than anything else, an act of imagination?

Further Reading:

  • Chan, S., Pataranutaporn, P., Suri, A., Zulfikar, W., Maes, P., and Loftus, E.F.
    Conversational AI powered by large language models amplifies false memories in witness interviews. (2024). arXiv:2408.04681v1. 10.48550/arXiv.2408.04681
  • Dick, P.K. 
    “We can remember it for you wholesale.” In Selected Stories of Philip K. Dick, J. Lethem (Ed.), 248–268 (2002). Random House, New York, NY.
  • Lamport, L.
    Time, clocks, and the ordering of events in a distributed system. Communications of the ACM 21, 7 (July 1978), 558–565. 10.1145/359545.359563
  • Pataranutaporn, P., Archiwaranguprok, C., Chan, S.W.T., Loftus, E., and Maes, P.
    Synthetic human memories: AI-edited images and videos can implant false memories and distort recollection. In Proceedings of the 2025 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (2025). Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, Article 538, 1–20. 10.1145/3706598.3713697
  • Popovski, P.
    How AI’s sense of time will differ from ours. IEEE Spectrum 13 August (2025). https://spectrum.ieee.org/ai-perception-of-time
  • Schacter, D.L.
    The seven sins of memory: Insights from psychology and cognitive neuroscience. American Psychologist 54, 3, 182–203 (1999). 10.1037/0003-066X.54.3.182
